metadata.title: Hidden Heritage London — Blue Plaques Dataset (Sample)
metadata.source: English Heritage Blue Plaques Scheme (factual location data) + project-augmented fields
metadata.note: environment_score, visitor_density, opening_status and routes are simulated/curated for prototype purposes. In a production version they would be sourced from OS Open Roads, Strava heatmaps, EH/Historic England opening times API and curator review.

properties.name: Charles Dickens House
properties.description: Dickens lived here 1837–1839 and wrote Oliver Twist and Nicholas Nickleby. Now the Charles Dickens Museum.

properties.name: Virginia Woolf at Fitzroy Square
properties.description: Woolf lived here 1907–1911 with her brother Adrian, hosting early Bloomsbury Group meetings. George Bernard Shaw lived in the same house earlier.
properties.opening_info: Plaque visible from the street at all times. Building is private.

properties.name: W. B. Yeats at Woburn Walk
properties.description: Irish poet Yeats lived here 1895–1919 in one of London's earliest pedestrianised shopping streets.

properties.name: T. S. Eliot at Russell Square
properties.description: Eliot worked here as an editor at Faber & Faber from 1925 until his death in 1965.

properties.name: Jimi Hendrix at Brook Street
properties.description: Hendrix lived in the upper-floor flat 1968–1969. Next door (no. 25) is the Handel House — Handel lived there in the 18th century. Both are now the Handel & Hendrix museum.

properties.name: Karl Marx at Dean Street
properties.description: Marx lived in two cramped rooms here 1851–1856 with his family while researching Das Kapital at the British Museum.
properties.opening_info: Plaque visible from the street. Building now houses Quo Vadis restaurant.

properties.name: Mozart in London
properties.description: The 8-year-old Mozart composed his first symphony here in 1764 during his family's London tour.

properties.name: Oscar Wilde at Tite Street
properties.description: Wilde lived here 1884–1895, writing The Picture of Dorian Gray and The Importance of Being Earnest, until his arrest.

properties.name: James McNeill Whistler at Tite Street
properties.description: American painter Whistler lived here 1866–1878 and painted his famous 'Nocturnes' series of the Thames at twilight.

properties.name: Sigmund Freud House
properties.description: Freud spent the last year of his life here after fleeing Nazi Vienna. His study is preserved exactly as he left it.

properties.name: Alan Turing in Maida Vale
properties.description: Turing was born here in 1912. The pioneering mathematician and codebreaker is now considered the father of theoretical computer science.

properties.name: Charles Darwin at Gower Street
properties.address: Biological Sciences Building, UCL, Gower Street, WC1E
properties.description: Darwin's house at 110 Gower Street stood on this site 1838–1842. He wrote much of his early evolutionary theory here before moving to Down House.
properties.opening_info: Plaque on the modern UCL building.

properties.name: Florence Nightingale's Last Home
properties.description: Nightingale lived here from 1865 until her death in 1910, running her nursing reforms by correspondence from her sickbed.

properties.name: Mary Wollstonecraft & Mary Shelley
properties.description: Wollstonecraft, author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, lived here 1788–1791. Her daughter Mary Shelley would later write Frankenstein.

properties.name: Vincent van Gogh in Brixton
properties.description: Van Gogh lived in this terraced house 1873–1874 as a 20-year-old art dealer, long before he became a painter.
properties.opening_info: Open occasionally as 'Van Gogh House London' — book ahead.

properties.name: Mahatma Gandhi in Barons Court
properties.description: Gandhi lived here as a law student 1888–1891. Cited as a quiet residential plaque tourists rarely see.

properties.name: Hyde Park Pet Cemetery
properties.description: A tiny Victorian pet cemetery with ~300 pet graves, hidden behind the Victoria Lodge. Established 1881, closed 1903. Almost no tourists know it exists.
properties.opening_info: Royal Parks group tours only — book months ahead.

properties.name: Postman's Park & Watts Memorial
properties.address: Postman's Park, King Edward Street, EC1A
properties.description: A small City of London garden containing the Memorial to Heroic Self-Sacrifice — 54 ceramic plaques commemorating ordinary people who died saving others' lives.
properties.opening_info: Open dawn to dusk daily, year-round.

properties.name: Crossbones Graveyard
properties.description: A medieval unconsecrated burial ground for sex workers ('Winchester Geese') and the poor of Southwark. Now a community garden of remembrance.
properties.opening_info: Garden open Wed–Fri 12:00–14:00. Vigil 7pm on the 23rd of each month.

properties.name: Leinster Gardens Fake Houses
properties.description: Two five-storey Victorian house facades with no building behind them, built in 1868 to hide a Metropolitan Line vent. Look up — windows are painted on.
properties.opening_info: Visible from the street at all times.

properties.name: Hampstead Heath Bathing Ponds
properties.description: Three Victorian-era natural swimming ponds (Men's, Ladies', Mixed) fed by the source of the River Fleet. A protected open-water swimming heritage site.
properties.opening_info: Open year-round 7:00–dusk. £4.50 entry. Quietest in early mornings.

properties.name: Hidden River Effra Manhole
properties.description: One of the few audible points where you can still hear the buried River Effra flowing beneath Brixton on its way to the Thames.

properties.name: Roman Bath in Strand Lane
properties.description: A small spring-fed bath of disputed origin (Roman, Tudor or 17th century) hidden in an alley off the Strand. National Trust managed.
properties.opening_info: Wed afternoons May–Sept by appointment only. Free.

properties.name: St Dunstan-in-the-East Church Garden
properties.description: A Wren church bombed in the Blitz, now a public garden inside the medieval ruins. One of the City's most peaceful spaces.

properties.name: Bunhill Fields Burial Ground
properties.description: A nonconformist burial ground containing the graves of John Bunyan, Daniel Defoe and William Blake. Calmest at lunchtime midweek.

properties.name: Benjamin Franklin House
properties.description: Franklin lived here 1757–1775 — the only surviving Franklin residence in the world.

properties.name: John Singer Sargent at Tite Street
properties.description: American portrait painter Sargent lived and worked in this Chelsea studio house from 1885 until his death.

properties.name: Bram Stoker at Cheyne Walk
properties.description: Author of Dracula, Stoker lived here 1896–1906 while managing the Lyceum Theatre.

properties.name: George Eliot at Cheyne Walk
properties.description: Marian Evans (George Eliot) moved into this house with her husband John Cross in December 1880 — and died here three weeks later.

properties.name: Dadabhai Naoroji
properties.description: First Asian MP in the UK Parliament, elected 1892. Plaque marks his residence.

properties.name: Keats House
properties.description: Keats lived here 1818–1820 and wrote 'Ode to a Nightingale' in the garden. Now a museum.

properties.name: John Logie Baird & First TV Demo
properties.description: Baird gave the world's first demonstration of television here in 1926, in a top-floor laboratory above what is now Bar Italia.

properties.name: Edward Elgar in West Kensington
properties.description: Elgar lived here 1890–1891 — early years of his composing career, before fame.

properties.name: Frédéric Chopin's Last Concert
properties.description: Chopin stayed here in 1848, gave his last public concert in London at this house, and died the following year.

properties.name: Lenin in Bloomsbury
properties.description: Lenin stayed here in 1908 while researching at the British Museum and editing Iskra, the Bolshevik newspaper.

properties.name: Sun Yat-sen in Holborn
properties.description: The founder of the Republic of China was kidnapped from a London street near here by the Qing legation in 1896.

properties.name: Sylvia Plath at Chalcot Square
properties.description: Plath lived here with Ted Hughes 1960–1961. She wrote The Bell Jar partly in this flat.

properties.name: George Orwell in Canonbury
properties.description: Orwell lived here 1944–1947, where he wrote Animal Farm. He left for Jura to write Nineteen Eighty-Four.

properties.name: H.G. Wells at Hanover Terrace
properties.description: Wells lived in this Nash terrace from 1936 until his death — refusing to leave during the Blitz.

properties.name: Beatrix Potter at Bolton Gardens
properties.description: Potter was born here in 1866 and lived in this house for nearly 50 years before moving to the Lake District.
properties.opening_info: Original house bombed in WWII; plaque on the rebuilt school on the site.

properties.name: George Frideric Handel House
properties.description: Handel lived and composed here 1723–1759, including Messiah. Now joined to the Hendrix flat next door as one museum.

properties.name: Dr Johnson's House
properties.description: Johnson compiled his Dictionary of the English Language in the garret of this house 1748–1755.

properties.name: Sir John Soane's Museum
properties.description: Soane left his house and collection to the nation on condition nothing be changed. A bewildering labyrinth of antiquities and Hogarth's Rake's Progress.

properties.name: Charles Babbage at Dorset Street
properties.description: Babbage lived here from 1829 until his death — designing his Analytical Engine, the conceptual ancestor of the computer.

properties.name: Michael Faraday at Blandford Street
properties.description: Faraday was apprenticed to a bookbinder here as a teenager. He read every science book that came in for binding — and the rest is electromagnetism.

properties.name: Ada Lovelace at St James's Square
properties.description: The world's first computer programmer wrote her notes on Babbage's Analytical Engine while living here.

properties.name: Mary Seacole at Soho Square
properties.description: Jamaican-born nurse who funded her own passage to the Crimea after the War Office turned her down. Lived here briefly between her travels.

properties.name: Emmeline Pankhurst at Clarendon Road
properties.description: The leader of the Suffragettes lived here when the WSPU's London militancy was at its height.

properties.name: Octavia Hill at Garbutt Place
properties.description: Hill bought these slum houses in 1864 to begin her experiments in social housing reform — the seed of the National Trust she co-founded.

properties.name: Joseph Bazalgette in St John's Wood
properties.description: The engineer of London's sewers lived here. His system, designed after the 1858 Great Stink, still serves the city today.

properties.name: Isambard Kingdom Brunel at Cheyne Walk
properties.description: Brunel lived in this riverside house from 1808 (with his father) and again 1857–1859 — overlooking the Thames he engineered tunnels under.

properties.name: Brunel Museum & Thames Tunnel
properties.description: The world's first under-river tunnel (1825–1843), now a museum inside the original engine house. The shaft is open for atmospheric concerts.

properties.name: Wilton's Music Hall
properties.description: The world's oldest surviving grand music hall (1859), tucked behind a row of houses. Astonishing peeling-paint interior, still hosts shows.
properties.opening_info: Bar Mon–Sat from 17:00. Free building tours occasionally.

properties.name: Dennis Severs' House
properties.description: A still-life-drama house museum recreating the lives of an imaginary Huguenot silk-weaver family across 200 years. Visited in silence.
properties.opening_info: Mon, Wed, Fri evenings; Sun afternoons. Pre-book. £20.

properties.name: Highgate Cemetery (East)
properties.description: Karl Marx's tomb, George Eliot, Douglas Adams, Jeremy Beadle. The east side is the wilder, atmospheric one — open for self-guided walks.

properties.name: Brompton Cemetery
properties.description: One of the 'Magnificent Seven' Victorian cemeteries. Beatrix Potter borrowed character names from headstones here. Free, leafy, atmospheric.

properties.name: St Pancras Old Church & Hardy Tree
properties.description: One of the oldest sites of Christian worship in England. The 'Hardy Tree' in the churchyard is encircled by gravestones rearranged by a young Thomas Hardy.
properties.opening_info: Churchyard open daily, free. Note: the Hardy Tree fell in 2022 — the gravestones remain.

properties.name: Chelsea Physic Garden
properties.description: London's oldest botanic garden, founded 1673 by the Society of Apothecaries. A walled microclimate hidden behind tall walls.

properties.name: Barbican Conservatory
properties.description: London's second-largest conservatory, with 2,000+ tropical species hidden inside the brutalist Barbican. Most Londoners have never been inside.
properties.opening_info: Open most Sundays + bank holidays 12:00–17:00. Free, book ahead.

properties.name: Phoenix Garden
properties.description: A community garden built on a wartime bombsite-turned-carpark, run by volunteers. Sits a minute from Cambridge Circus but feels miles away.

properties.name: Camley Street Natural Park
properties.description: A wildlife reserve squeezed between the Regent's Canal and the railway lines behind King's Cross. Reed beds, kingfishers and frogs in the heart of zone 1.

properties.name: Inner Temple Garden
properties.description: A barristers' garden going back to the 12th-century Knights Templar. The supposed setting where the rival roses of York and Lancaster were first plucked.
properties.opening_info: Open weekday lunchtimes (12:30–15:00) most of the year. Free.

properties.name: The Black Friar
properties.description: A 1905 Arts & Crafts pub built into a wedge-shaped Victorian building. The interior is a fevered Pre-Raphaelite vision of Dominican friars in marble and bronze.

properties.name: Goodwin's Court
properties.description: A bow-fronted 17th-century alley said to have inspired Diagon Alley. Easy to walk past on St Martin's Lane.
properties.opening_info: Visible from the street at all times.

properties.name: Pickering Place
properties.description: London's smallest public square, reached through a wood-panelled alley beside Berry Bros. & Rudd. Site of the last duels in England.
properties.opening_info: Visible from the alley at all times.

properties.name: Smithfield Market
properties.description: London's last remaining wholesale meat market, in a Grade II listed Victorian iron and glass hall by Horace Jones.
properties.opening_info: Trading 02:00–07:00 Mon–Fri. Visit in mornings to see the market in action.

properties.name: Old Operating Theatre Museum
properties.description: The oldest surviving operating theatre in Europe (1822), in the herb garret of an old St Thomas's hospital church. Reached by a narrow spiral stair.

properties.name: 2 Willow Road (Goldfinger House)
properties.description: A 1939 modernist house designed by Goldfinger as his own home, preserved with original furniture, art and family belongings. National Trust.

properties.name: Burgh House Hampstead
properties.description: A Queen Anne house (1704) housing a small Hampstead history museum. Free entry, with a buttery in the basement.

properties.name: Wapping Old Stairs
properties.description: A medieval watermen's stair down to the Thames foreshore, beside the Town of Ramsgate pub where Captain Bligh and Judge Jeffreys both drank.
properties.opening_info: Foreshore accessible at low tide only. Check tide times.
